{"step_by_step_thinking": "The patient presents with central, constant chest pain unrelated to exertion, which worsens when lying down and improves with leaning forward. This is a classic description of pericarditis. The physical exam finding of a scratching sound (friction rub) audible over the left sternal border at end-expiration with the patient leaning forward further supports the diagnosis of pericarditis. The pain radiates to the shoulders and neck, which is consistent with pericardial involvement. The chest X-ray is normal, which helps rule out pulmonary causes, and the ECG, though not described in detail, would typically show diffuse ST elevations in pericarditis. The patient has no significant past medical history, and smoking is a risk factor for cardiovascular disease but not specific to pericarditis. Treatment for pericarditis typically includes nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as indomethacin or ibuprofen to reduce inflammation and relieve pain. Colchicine is often added to NSAIDs in recurrent or refractory cases to reduce the risk of recurrence. Omeprazole is sometimes used to prevent gastric irritation from NSAIDs. Pericardiocentesis or pericardiectomy are invasive procedures reserved for complications such as cardiac tamponade or refractory pericarditis, which are not indicated here. Given the presentation and lack of signs of tamponade or severe complications, the optimal therapy is medical management with NSAIDs and colchicine. Option B (Ibuprofen + colchicine +/- omeprazole) is the most appropriate choice.", "answer_choice": "B"}
